title: An Email from Grandma (電子郵件)
passage:
Dear Anna,
Thank you so much for the lovely card you made for my birthday. It is now on my fridge, and I smile every time I see it. Your drawing of our cat, Lucky, is wonderful!
I heard from your mom that you won first prize in the science fair. I am so proud of you! When you visit next month, you must tell me all about your project.
The weather here is getting cold, so remember to bring a warm coat. I can't wait to see you and bake cookies together.
Love,
Grandma

questions.question: What is the main purpose of Grandma's email?
options: (A) To invite Anna to come and live with her
(B) To thank Anna for the card and say she looks forward to the visit
(C) To complain about the cold weather
(D) To teach Anna how to do a science project
questions.explanation: 信中先感謝生日卡，又說期待下個月相見、一起烤餅乾，主要目的為感謝並表達期待，選 B。
optionsZh: 邀請 Anna 搬來同住
感謝 Anna 的卡片並說期待見面
教 Anna 怎麼做科展

questions.question: What can we learn from the email?
options: (A) Anna won first prize in the science fair
(B) Grandma does not like the cat
(C) Anna is going to visit this week
(D) Grandma lives in a warm place
questions.explanation: 信中提到 you won first prize in the science fair，故選 A；探訪是下個月、當地天氣轉冷，其餘選項與內文不符。
optionsZh: Anna 在科展得了第一名

title: The Lost Wallet (故事)
passage:
On his way home from school, Kevin saw a brown wallet lying on the sidewalk. He picked it up and looked inside. There was some money, a student ID card, and a photo of a smiling family. The name on the card was "Daniel Wu."
Kevin thought for a moment. He could keep the money and no one would know. But then he looked at the happy family in the photo and imagined how worried Daniel must be. He decided to do the right thing.
Using the ID card, Kevin found out which school Daniel went to. The next morning, he took the wallet to that school office. An hour later, Daniel came to thank him with tears in his eyes. "My grandfather gave me that photo," he said. "I thought I had lost it forever."
Kevin felt warm inside. He realized that helping others made him much happier than money ever could.

questions.question: What was inside the wallet?
options: (A) Money, a student ID card, and a family photo
(B) A key and a pen
(C) A cellphone
(D) A letter and some stamps
questions.explanation: 第一段提到 money、a student ID card、a photo of a smiling family，故選 A。
optionsZh: 錢、學生證和一張全家福

questions.question: Why did Daniel cry when he got his wallet back?
options: (A) He was scolded by his teacher
(B) He got back the photo his grandfather gave him
(C) He lost a lot of money
(D) He was late for school
questions.explanation: Daniel 說那張照片是爺爺給的、本以為永遠弄丟了，感動落淚，故選 B。
optionsZh: 他拿回了爺爺給他的照片

questions.question: What can we learn from this story?
options: (A) You should spend money you find right away
(B) You should never talk to strangers
(C) Helping others brings more happiness than money
(D) You should not bring a wallet to school
questions.explanation: 末段 Kevin 體會到幫助他人比金錢更讓他快樂，這是全文主旨，故選 C。
optionsZh: 幫助別人帶來的快樂勝過金錢

title: Why Do We Dream? (說明文)
passage:
Every night, while we sleep, our brains create stories called dreams. Some dreams are happy, some are strange, and some are scary. For thousands of years, people have wondered why we dream. Even today, scientists do not have a single perfect answer, but they have some interesting ideas.
One idea is that dreams help us deal with our feelings. During the day, we may feel worried, excited, or sad. When we dream, our brain sorts through these feelings, almost like cleaning a messy room. This may be why we often feel better after a good night's sleep.
Another idea is that dreams help us remember. While we sleep, the brain reviews what we learned during the day. It keeps the important information and throws away what we do not need. Students who sleep well often remember their lessons better than those who stay up late.
Some scientists also believe that dreaming lets us practice. In dreams, we might solve problems or face our fears in a safe way. A child who dreams about giving a speech may feel braver when doing it in real life.
We may never fully understand our dreams. But one thing is clear: dreaming is an important part of being human, and a good night's sleep helps both our minds and our bodies.

questions.question: What is this passage mainly about?
options: (A) How to sleep for a longer time
(B) Some ideas about why people dream
(C) How to remember bad dreams
(D) Why students should not stay up late
questions.explanation: 全文圍繞「我們為什麼做夢」介紹科學家的幾種推測，故主旨為 B。
optionsZh: 關於人為何做夢的一些看法

questions.question: According to the passage, how might dreaming help us remember?
options: (A) The brain reviews what we learned and keeps the important information
(B) The brain completely stops working during sleep
(C) It makes us forget everything we learned
(D) It makes us need less sleep
questions.explanation: 第三段說睡覺時大腦會複習白天所學、保留重要資訊，故選 A。
optionsZh: 大腦複習所學並保留重要資訊
睡覺時大腦完全停止運作
它讓我們忘記所學的一切
它讓我們需要更少睡眠

questions.question: What does "sorts through" in the second paragraph most likely mean?
options: (A) Throws all away
(B) Organizes
(C) Creates
(D) Hides
questions.explanation: 後面用 like cleaning a messy room（像整理凌亂的房間）作比喻，可推知 sorts through 意為「整理」，選 B。

title: The Honeybee's Secret Language (說明文)
passage:
Bees are some of the hardest workers in nature. They visit hundreds of flowers every day to collect nectar, which they turn into honey. But what makes bees truly amazing is the way they talk to each other.
When a honeybee finds a good source of food, it flies back to the hive and performs a special dance. Scientists call this the "waggle dance." The direction of the dance tells other bees which way to fly. The length of the dance shows how far away the flowers are. The longer the dance, the farther the flowers.
This dance language was first discovered by Austrian scientist Karl von Frisch in the 1940s. He spent years watching bees and finally understood their secret. In 1973, he won the Nobel Prize for this discovery.
Without bees, many plants could not grow. Bees carry pollen from flower to flower, which helps plants make seeds. If bees disappeared, many fruits and vegetables we eat every day would also disappear. Protecting bees means protecting our food.

questions.question: What does the length of the waggle dance show?
options: (A) The type of flower the bee found
(B) The amount of nectar in the flower
(C) How far away the food source is
(D) How many bees should fly together
questions.explanation: 第二段明確指出 The length of the dance shows how far away the flowers are，故選 C。

questions.question: Why did Karl von Frisch win the Nobel Prize?
options: (A) He invented a new type of honey
(B) He discovered how bees communicate through dance
(C) He found a way to save bees from danger
(D) He grew a new kind of flower for bees
questions.explanation: 第三段說他花了多年觀察蜜蜂、弄懂牠們的舞蹈語言，因此在 1973 年獲諾貝爾獎，選 B。
optionsZh: 他發明了一種新型蜂蜜
他發現了蜜蜂用舞蹈溝通的方式
他找到拯救蜜蜂的方法
他為蜜蜂種了一種新花

questions.question: What does the writer suggest in the last paragraph?
options: (A) Bees should be kept as pets
(B) We should eat less honey to protect bees
(C) Bees play an important role in helping our food supply
(D) Bees can survive without flowers
questions.explanation: 末段說蜜蜂幫助植物結種、若蜜蜂消失許多蔬果也會消失，暗示保護蜜蜂就是保護糧食，選 C。
optionsZh: 蜜蜂應該被當成寵物飼養
我們應少吃蜂蜜來保護蜜蜂
蜜蜂在糧食供應中扮演重要角色

title: Should Students Use Phones in Class? — Two Views (雙方觀點)
passage:
■ View A — By Cindy Chen, Grade 9 Student

I think students should be allowed to use phones in class. Phones are powerful learning tools. With a phone, I can look up words I do not know, watch short videos to understand difficult ideas, and take photos of the board instead of copying everything by hand. Many schools around the world already allow phones, and their students do just fine. The problem is not the phone — it is how we use it. If teachers set clear rules, phones can help us learn more effectively.

■ View B — By Mr. David Lin, English Teacher

I disagree. In my experience, phones are one of the biggest distractions in the classroom. When students have their phones, they check social media, play games, and stop paying attention. Even when they plan to use the phone for learning, they easily get pulled into other apps. Young people's brains are still developing, and they find it hard to resist the temptation. Schools should be places of focused learning, and phones make that very difficult to achieve.

questions.question: What is Cindy's main argument for allowing phones in class?
options: (A) Phones are cheaper than textbooks
(B) Phones can be useful learning tools if used correctly
(C) All schools in the world allow phones
(D) Phones help students make new friends
questions.explanation: Cindy 認為手機是強大的學習工具，只要老師設定規則就能幫助學習，故選 B；「所有學校」過於絕對，A 不在文中。
optionsZh: 手機若使用得當可以是有用的學習工具
全世界所有學校都允許帶手機
手機幫助學生交到新朋友

questions.question: What does Mr. Lin say about young people's brains?
options: (A) They learn faster with phones
(B) They are still developing and find it hard to resist temptation
(C) They do not need phones to succeed
(D) They are better at multitasking than adults
questions.explanation: Mr. Lin 說年輕人的大腦還在發育，很難抵抗誘惑（find it hard to resist the temptation），故選 B。
optionsZh: 仍在發育中，很難抵抗誘惑

(B) Phones can be connected to the internet

questions.explanation: Cindy 強調「使用方式」（how we use it）是關鍵；Mr. Lin 也認為問題在於學生「如何使用」（easily get pulled into other apps）。兩人都關注使用方式的問題，故選 C。
optionsZh: 手機應在所有學校禁用
如何使用手機是重要議題

title: Grandpa's Radio (故事)
passage:
Every summer, Sam visited his grandfather in the countryside. Grandpa's house was old and quiet, without a TV or computer. The only thing that made noise was a small brown radio on the kitchen shelf.
One rainy afternoon, Sam was bored and picked up the radio. As he turned the dial, he heard music that sounded like it came from another time. "That's jazz," said Grandpa, walking in from the garden. "My father used to play it at dances when I was your age."
For the rest of the afternoon, Grandpa told Sam stories while the radio played softly in the background. Sam heard about family members he had never met and places he had never seen. He forgot all about his phone and the games waiting at home.
When it was time to leave, Sam asked if he could have the radio. Grandpa smiled and shook his head. "It belongs here," he said. "But you can come back and listen anytime." And Sam did — every summer until the radio finally fell silent, years later.

questions.question: What did Sam and Grandpa do on the rainy afternoon?
options: (A) They watched TV together
(B) They played games on Sam's phone
(C) Grandpa told Sam family stories while the radio played
(D) They went to a jazz concert in town
questions.explanation: 第三段說 Grandpa told Sam stories while the radio played softly，故選 C。
optionsZh: 他們玩 Sam 的手機遊戲
爺爺一邊放著廣播一邊講家族故事
他們去城鎮看爵士音樂會

questions.question: Why did Grandpa refuse to give Sam the radio?
options: (A) The radio was too expensive to give away
(B) Grandpa did not want Sam to have it
(C) Grandpa said the radio belonged where it was
(D) Sam did not ask for it politely
questions.explanation: 爺爺說 It belongs here，表示廣播機屬於那個地方，選 C；他仍然歡迎 Sam 回來聽，並非不想給，選 B 不準確。
optionsZh: 爺爺說廣播機屬於那裡

questions.question: What does this story mainly show?
options: (A) Old radios sound better than modern music players
(B) Simple things can bring people closer and create lasting memories
(C) Children should spend more time in the countryside
(D) Jazz music is more popular than other types of music
questions.explanation: 故事透過廣播機連接祖孫兩代、讓 Sam 每年回訪直到廣播沉默，主旨是簡單的事物可以拉近人心並留下深刻回憶，選 B。
optionsZh: 老廣播機比現代音樂播放器音質更好
簡單的事物能拉近人心並留下長久回憶
孩子應該在鄉下待更多時間
爵士樂比其他類型的音樂更受歡迎

title: Why Do Cats Sleep So Much? (說明文)
passage:
Cats are famous for sleeping a lot. Most cats sleep for twelve to sixteen hours every day. Some cats even sleep for twenty hours! This might seem strange to us, but there is a good reason.
Long ago, wild cats hunted small animals for food. Hunting takes a lot of energy. To save energy for hunting, cats' bodies learned to rest often. Even pet cats today still have this habit.
Cats also sleep more when they feel safe. A cat that trusts its home will sleep deeply. If a cat feels nervous, it may sleep less and stay alert instead.
So the next time you see a cat sleeping, remember: it is not lazy. It is simply following an old habit from its wild past.

questions.question: How many hours do most cats sleep every day?
options: (A) 2 to 4 hours
(B) 6 to 8 hours
(C) 12 to 16 hours

options: (A) To grow bigger and stronger
(B) To save energy for hunting
(C) To avoid getting sick
(D) To stay away from other cats

optionsZh: 為了保留獵食所需的能量

title: The Story of Bubble Tea (說明文)
passage:
Bubble tea is one of Taiwan's most famous drinks. It was created in Taichung in the 1980s. A tea shop owner had an idea: add small, chewy balls made from tapioca to sweet milk tea.
The little balls are called "pearls." People drink bubble tea with a big straw so the pearls can pass through easily. Today, bubble tea shops can be found all over the world, from Japan to the United States.
Many people enjoy choosing their own sugar level and ice level. This makes each cup a little different. Bubble tea shows how a simple idea from Taiwan can become popular everywhere.

questions.question: Where was bubble tea created?
options: (A) Taipei
(B) Taichung
(C) Tokyo
(D) New York
questions.explanation: 文中提到 It was created in Taichung in the 1980s，珍珠奶茶誕生於台中，故選 B。

questions.question: Why do people use a big straw to drink bubble tea?
options: (A) So the tea stays hot longer
(B) So the pearls can pass through easily
(C) So the drink looks more colorful
(D) So less sugar is needed
questions.explanation: 文中說 People drink bubble tea with a big straw so the pearls can pass through easily，粗吸管是為了讓珍珠順利通過，故選 B。

title: A New Student's First Day (故事)
passage:
Today was Mia's first day at a new school. She did not know anyone, and she felt nervous walking into the classroom. She sat alone at the back and looked at her desk.
During lunch, a girl named Ellie came over and smiled. "Do you want to sit with us?" Ellie asked. Mia felt happy and said yes right away.
They talked about their favorite shows and games. Mia learned that Ellie also liked drawing, just like her. By the end of the day, Mia had made her first new friend.
Walking home, Mia smiled to herself. She was no longer worried about tomorrow.

questions.question: What did Ellie do at lunch?
options: (A) She ignored Mia
(B) She asked Mia to sit with her
(C) She left the classroom early
(D) She gave Mia her lunch
questions.explanation: 文中寫 Ellie asked, "Do you want to sit with us?"，Ellie 主動邀請 Mia 一起坐，故選 B。

questions.question: How did Mia feel by the end of the day?
options: (A) Nervous about tomorrow
(B) Angry at Ellie
(C) Less worried and happy
(D) Tired and bored
questions.explanation: 文末寫 Mia smiled...she was no longer worried about tomorrow，顯示她變得開心、不再擔心，故選 C。

title: The Kind Bus Driver (故事)
passage:
Every morning, Tony took the same bus to school. The driver, Mr. Wang, always smiled and said hello to every student. One cold morning, Tony forgot his bus card at home.
Tony felt worried. He did not have any money either. Mr. Wang saw his face and said, "Don't worry. You can pay me tomorrow." Tony was surprised and thanked him many times.
The next day, Tony paid Mr. Wang double the fare to say thank you. Mr. Wang laughed and gave the extra money back. "Just be kind to someone else someday," he said.
Tony never forgot those words.

questions.question: What problem did Tony have one cold morning?
options: (A) He missed the bus
(B) He forgot his bus card and had no money
(C) He was late for school
(D) He lost his backpack
questions.explanation: 文中提到 Tony forgot his bus card at home... He did not have any money either，說明他忘帶卡又沒錢，故選 B。
optionsZh: 他忘記帶公車卡且沒有錢

questions.question: What lesson does Mr. Wang want Tony to learn?
options: (A) Always carry extra money
(B) Wake up earlier every day
(C) Pass kindness on to others
(D) Never trust strangers
questions.explanation: 王先生說 Just be kind to someone else someday，希望 Tony 也能把善意傳給別人，故選 C。

title: Planning a Weekend Hike (對話)
caption:
Amy: Hey! Want to go hiking this Saturday?
Ben: Sure! Which trail should we go to?
Amy: How about Elephant Mountain? The view of the city is amazing.
Ben: Sounds great. What time should we meet?
Amy: Let's meet at the MRT station at 8 a.m. Don't forget water and sunscreen!
Ben: Got it. Should I bring snacks too?
Amy: Yes, please! I'll bring some fruit.

questions.question: Where do Amy and Ben plan to meet?
options: (A) At Elephant Mountain
(B) At the MRT station
(C) At Ben's house
(D) At a restaurant
questions.explanation: Amy 說 Let's meet at the MRT station at 8 a.m.，兩人約在捷運站見面，故選 B。

questions.question: What did Ben offer to bring on the hike?
options: (A) Water
(B) Sunscreen
(C) Snacks
(D) A tent
questions.explanation: Ben 問 Should I bring snacks too? Amy 回答 Yes, please!，表示 Ben 主動提議帶點心，故選 C。

title: Community Garden Volunteers Needed (公告)
passage:

title: How Octopuses Change Color (說明文)
passage:
Octopuses are famous for their amazing ability to change color. Within a second, an octopus can shift from bright red to pale gray, or even match the pattern of the rocks around it.
This skill comes from special cells in the octopus's skin called chromatophores. Each of these tiny cells holds colored pigment. When an octopus wants to change color, its brain sends a signal, and the cells stretch or shrink to show more or less color.
Octopuses use this ability for two main reasons. First, it helps them hide from hungry predators like sharks. Second, it allows them to sneak up on small fish and crabs without being noticed.
Scientists are still studying how octopuses control this process so quickly, hoping the knowledge might one day help create new kinds of color-changing materials.

questions.question: What are the special skin cells that let octopuses change color called?
options: (A) Neurons
(B) Chromatophores
(C) Tentacles
(D) Pigments only
questions.explanation: 文中提到 special cells in the octopus's skin called chromatophores，這些皮膚細胞稱為色素細胞，故選 B。

questions.question: Why do octopuses change color, according to the passage?
options: (A) To attract other octopuses only
(B) To hide from predators and catch prey
(C) To stay warm in cold water
(D) To communicate with humans
questions.explanation: 文中說變色能幫助 hide from hungry predators 及 sneak up on small fish and crabs，故選 B。
optionsZh: 為了躲避掠食者並捕捉獵物

questions.question: What does "predators" most likely mean in the second paragraph?
options: (A) Animals that hunt other animals for food
(B) Animals that live in groups
(C) Animals that cannot swim
(D) Animals that are afraid of light
questions.explanation: 段落提到 hide from hungry predators like sharks，鯊魚是掠食者，可推知 predators 指獵食其他動物的動物，故選 A。
optionsZh: 會獵捕其他動物作為食物的動物

title: The Invention of the Umbrella (說明文)
passage:
Umbrellas seem like a simple invention, but their history is thousands of years old. The earliest umbrellas were not used for rain at all — they were used to block the hot sun.
In ancient Egypt and China, umbrellas were often carried by servants to protect kings and nobles from sunlight. Owning an umbrella was actually a sign of high status, not something for everyday people.
It took a long time before umbrellas became waterproof and common for rainy weather. In the 1700s, a British man named Jonas Hanway carried an umbrella in the rain in London. At first, people laughed at him because only women used umbrellas at that time. But slowly, more men started copying him.
Today, umbrellas are used by almost everyone, and no one thinks twice about carrying one on a rainy day.

questions.question: What were the earliest umbrellas mainly used for?
options: (A) Blocking rain
(B) Blocking sunlight
(C) Decorating houses
(D) Carrying food
questions.explanation: 文中說 they were used to block the hot sun，最早的雨傘其實是用來遮陽，故選 B。

questions.question: Why did people laugh at Jonas Hanway?
options: (A) He dropped his umbrella in public
(B) He used an umbrella, which only women did then
(C) He sold umbrellas for too much money
(D) He refused to go outside in the rain
questions.explanation: 文中說 people laughed at him because only women used umbrellas at that time，因為當時只有女性撐傘，故選 B。
optionsZh: 他在公開場合弄丟雨傘
他撐傘，而當時只有女性撐傘

questions.question: What does this passage mainly describe?
options: (A) How umbrellas are made today
(B) The history and changing use of umbrellas
(C) Why umbrellas break easily
(D) The most expensive umbrellas in the world
questions.explanation: 全文從遮陽用途講到防雨、從地位象徵到普及，主要描述雨傘的歷史演變，故選 B。
optionsZh: 雨傘的歷史與用途演變

title: The Music Box (故事)
passage:
When Lily was young, her grandmother gave her a small wooden music box. Every time Lily opened it, a gentle tune played, and a tiny ballerina spun slowly inside.
Years passed, and Lily grew up. She moved to a new city for work and packed the music box carefully in a box of old things. One evening, feeling homesick, she found the music box again.
As the familiar tune played, Lily suddenly remembered her grandmother's kitchen, the smell of fresh bread, and the sound of her laughter. She realized the music box was not just a toy — it carried memories she thought she had forgotten.
That night, Lily called her grandmother just to say she loved her.

questions.question: Who gave Lily the music box?
options: (A) Her mother
(B) Her teacher
(C) Her grandmother
(D) Her best friend
questions.explanation: 文中寫 her grandmother gave her a small wooden music box，音樂盒是祖母給的，故選 C。

questions.question: Why did Lily call her grandmother that night?
options: (A) She wanted to borrow money
(B) The music box brought back warm memories
(C) Her grandmother was sick
(D) She wanted to return the music box
questions.explanation: 音樂盒的旋律讓 Lily 想起祖母廚房的溫馨回憶，因而打電話表達思念，故選 B。
optionsZh: 音樂盒喚起了溫暖的回憶

questions.question: What does the music box represent in the story?
options: (A) A valuable antique worth a lot of money
(B) A reminder of Lily's childhood memories
(C) A gift Lily never liked
(D) A toy that no longer works
questions.explanation: 音樂盒承載著 Lily 童年與祖母相處的回憶，象徵意義大於物品本身價值，故選 B。

title: A Rainy Day Adventure (故事)
passage:
It was raining hard, and Jack and his little sister Emma were stuck inside all afternoon. "I'm so bored," Emma complained, staring out the window.
Jack had an idea. He grabbed some old blankets and chairs and built a fort in the living room. Emma's eyes lit up. Together, they made a "cave" with flashlights and pillows, pretending to be explorers searching for hidden treasure.
They found an old box of family photos under the sofa and used it as their "treasure." Emma laughed as Jack made up funny stories about each photo.
By the time their parents came home, the rain had stopped, but Jack and Emma didn't even notice. They had turned a boring rainy day into one of their favorite memories.

questions.question: What did Jack build to fix Emma's boredom?
options: (A) A sandcastle
(B) A fort in the living room
(C) A puzzle table
(D) A paper airplane
questions.explanation: 文中寫 Jack...built a fort in the living room，他在客廳搭建了堡壘，故選 B。

questions.question: What did they use as their "treasure"?
options: (A) A box of family photos
(B) Some old coins
(C) A box of toys
(D) A book of maps
questions.explanation: 文中提到 They found an old box of family photos...and used it as their "treasure"，他們把家庭照片當作寶藏，故選 A。

questions.question: What is this story mainly about?
options: (A) How to organize old photos
(B) Two children turning a boring day into a fun memory
(C) Why it rains so much in summer
(D) How to build a real tent outdoors
questions.explanation: 故事描述 Jack 和 Emma 靠想像力把無聊的雨天變成快樂回憶，故選 B。
optionsZh: 兩個孩子把無聊的一天變成有趣回憶
如何在戶外搭建真正的帳篷

title: Sunrise National Park — Camping Guidelines (公告)
passage:
Sunrise National Park — Camping Guidelines
Thank you for choosing Sunrise National Park for your camping trip. To keep the park safe and clean for everyone, please follow these rules carefully.
Before You Arrive:
• Reserve your campsite online at least three days before your trip.
• Check the weather forecast, since some trails close during heavy rain.
While Camping:
• Campfires are only allowed in the marked fire pits, and must be fully put out before you sleep or leave.
• Keep all food in sealed containers. Wild animals, especially bears, are attracted to food left outside.
• Quiet hours are from 10:00 p.m. to 6:00 a.m. Please keep noise low for other campers.
Before You Leave:
• Take all your trash with you. There are no trash cans in the camping area.
• Make sure your fire is completely cold before leaving your site.
Campers who break these rules may be asked to leave the park immediately and may not be allowed to return. Enjoy your time in nature, and please help us protect it for future visitors.

questions.question: Where are campers allowed to make a campfire?
options: (A) Anywhere inside their own campsite
(B) Only in the marked fire pits
(C) Only near the entrance gate
(D) Only during the day
questions.explanation: 規範寫 Campfires are only allowed in the marked fire pits，只能在標示的生火區升火，故選 B。

questions.question: Why should food be kept in sealed containers?
options: (A) To keep it warm
(B) To stop wild animals from being attracted to it
(C) To save space in the tent
(D) To follow the quiet hours rule
questions.explanation: 規範說 Wild animals, especially bears, are attracted to food left outside，密封食物是為防止野生動物靠近，故選 B。
optionsZh: 為了遵守安靜時段規定

questions.question: What should campers do with their trash before leaving?
options: (A) Burn it in the fire pit
(B) Bury it in the ground
(C) Take it with them
(D) Leave it in a sealed container
questions.explanation: 規範寫 Take all your trash with you. There are no trash cans in the camping area，露營區沒有垃圾桶，須自行帶走垃圾，故選 C。

title: Why Do Leaves Change Color in Fall? (說明文)
passage:
Every autumn, forests around the world transform into a beautiful mix of red, orange, and yellow. Many people enjoy traveling just to see this colorful show, but few know the science behind it.
During spring and summer, leaves are green because they are full of a substance called chlorophyll. Chlorophyll absorbs sunlight and helps trees make food through a process called photosynthesis. Because chlorophyll reflects green light, that is the color our eyes see.
As autumn arrives, days become shorter and temperatures drop. Trees sense these changes and begin to prepare for winter. They slow down photosynthesis and stop producing new chlorophyll. As the green chlorophyll breaks down, other colors that were hidden underneath, like yellow and orange, finally become visible.
Red colors are a little different. Some trees actually produce a brand-new red pigment in autumn, possibly to protect their leaves from strong sunlight while nutrients move back into the branches.
Eventually, the leaves dry out completely and fall to the ground, leaving the trees bare until spring returns the green once again.

questions.question: What does chlorophyll help trees do?
options: (A) Absorb sunlight and make food
(B) Grow taller in winter
(C) Change color in spring
(D) Attract more sunlight in autumn only
questions.explanation: 文中說 Chlorophyll absorbs sunlight and helps trees make food through...photosynthesis，葉綠素幫助樹木行光合作用製造養分，故選 A。
optionsZh: 只在秋天吸收更多陽光

questions.question: Why do yellow and orange colors appear in autumn leaves?
options: (A) Trees start producing new yellow pigment
(B) The green chlorophyll breaks down and reveals hidden colors
(C) Cold weather turns green pigment into yellow
(D) Sunlight bleaches the leaves white
questions.explanation: 文中說 As the green chlorophyll breaks down, other colors...become visible，黃橙色是葉綠素分解後顯現的原有色素，故選 B。
optionsZh: 樹木開始製造新的黃色色素
綠色葉綠素分解後顯現出原本隱藏的顏色
寒冷天氣把綠色素變成黃色
陽光把葉子漂白成白色

questions.question: According to the passage, why might trees produce red pigment in autumn?
options: (A) To attract more insects
(B) To protect leaves from strong sunlight
(C) To help photosynthesis work faster
(D) To keep leaves from falling
questions.explanation: 文中提到紅色素 possibly to protect their leaves from strong sunlight，可能是為了保護葉片免受強光傷害，故選 B。
optionsZh: 為了保護葉片不受強烈陽光傷害
為了讓光合作用更快進行

title: The Rise of Robot Waiters (說明文)
passage:
Walk into some restaurants today, and you might be served not by a person, but by a robot rolling smoothly between the tables. Robot waiters are becoming more and more common, especially in busy cities.
These robots usually follow a fixed path marked on the floor, carrying trays of food from the kitchen to the customer's table. Sensors help them avoid bumping into chairs, people, or other robots. Some advanced models can even say simple greetings like "Here is your order!"
Restaurant owners like robot waiters because they can work all day without getting tired, and they never forget an order. This is especially helpful when there are not enough workers to hire. However, robots cannot yet do everything a human server can do, such as chatting warmly with customers or handling unexpected problems.
For now, most restaurants use robots only for simple tasks, like carrying food, while human staff still take orders and solve problems. Some experts believe that in the future, robots and humans will work together even more closely, each doing what they are best at.

questions.question: How do robot waiters avoid bumping into people or chairs?
options: (A) By using sensors
(B) By following a worker's voice
(C) By moving very slowly at all times
(D) By stopping every few seconds
questions.explanation: 文中說 Sensors help them avoid bumping into chairs, people, or other robots，機器人靠感應器避免碰撞，故選 A。

questions.question: What is the purpose of the sentence "such as chatting warmly with customers or handling unexpected problems"?
options: (A) To list tasks robots can already do perfectly
(B) To give examples of things robots still cannot do well
(C) To explain how robots are programmed
(D) To describe a new robot model being tested
questions.explanation: 此句接在 robots cannot yet do everything a human server can do 之後，是舉例說明機器人尚無法勝任的工作，故選 B。
optionsZh: 列出機器人已經能完美完成的任務

解釋機器人如何被程式設計
描述一款正在測試的新機型

questions.question: Why do some restaurant owners like using robot waiters?
options: (A) Robots can work all day without getting tired
(B) Robots are cheaper to buy than plates
(C) Customers only want to be served by robots
(D) Robots can cook better than human chefs
questions.explanation: 文中說 they can work all day without getting tired, and they never forget an order，餐廳老闆喜歡機器人不會疲累的特點，故選 A。
optionsZh: 機器人可以整天工作而不會累
顧客只想要機器人服務
機器人比人類廚師更會做菜

title: The Photography Contest (故事)
passage:
Nathan had always loved taking photos, but he never thought his pictures were good enough to show anyone. When his teacher announced a school photography contest, his friends encouraged him to enter, but he still hesitated.
The night before the deadline, Nathan looked through hundreds of photos on his phone. Most were of ordinary things: his cat sleeping in the sun, raindrops on a window, his grandmother cooking dinner. He almost gave up, thinking none of them were special.
Finally, he chose a photo of his grandmother's hands kneading dough, flour dusting the kitchen table like snow. It felt personal and honest, even if it was not fancy.
Two weeks later, Nathan's photo won second place. The judges said it captured a quiet, real moment that most other entries lacked. Standing in front of the school, holding his small trophy, Nathan realized that a photo did not need to be perfect to be meaningful. It only needed to be true.

questions.question: What was Nathan's winning photo about?
options: (A) A sunset over the ocean
(B) His grandmother's hands kneading dough
(C) His cat sleeping in the sun
(D) Raindrops on a window
questions.explanation: 文中說他選了 a photo of his grandmother's hands kneading dough，得獎照片是祖母揉麵團的雙手，故選 B。

questions.question: Why did the judges like Nathan's photo?
options: (A) It used the most expensive camera equipment
(B) It showed a quiet, real, and honest moment
(C) It was the largest photo submitted
(D) It included the most people in one picture
questions.explanation: 評審說這張照片 captured a quiet, real moment that most other entries lacked，欣賞其真實安靜的氛圍，故選 B。
optionsZh: 使用了最昂貴的攝影器材
呈現了安靜、真實而誠懇的瞬間
是投稿中尺寸最大的照片

questions.question: What does Nathan learn by the end of the story?
options: (A) Only professional cameras can take good photos
(B) A photo does not need to be perfect to be meaningful
(C) Contests are not worth entering
(D) Family photos should never be shared publicly
questions.explanation: 文末寫 a photo did not need to be perfect to be meaningful. It only needed to be true，這是 Nathan 的體悟，故選 B。
optionsZh: 只有專業相機才能拍出好照片
照片不需要完美也能有意義
家庭照片不該公開分享

title: A Promise Kept (四格漫畫)
caption:
Panel 1: Leo lies on the sofa playing video games while his dog Max brings him a leash and stares at him hopefully.
Panel 2: Leo waves the dog away, saying, "Maybe later, Max," and keeps playing.
Panel 3: Max sadly walks away, and Leo suddenly notices the clock — it is already dark outside. He looks a little guilty.
Panel 4: Leo puts down the controller, grabs the leash himself, and they both run out the door together, both looking happy.

questions.question: What does the comic suggest happened between Panel 2 and Panel 3?
options: (A) Leo finished all his homework
(B) A long time passed while Leo kept playing games

title: Plastic Pollution in the Ocean (說明文)
passage:
Every year, millions of tons of plastic waste enter the world's oceans. Much of it comes from land — bottles, bags, and packaging that are thrown away carelessly and eventually washed into rivers and seas.
Unlike paper or food waste, plastic does not disappear quickly. Instead, sunlight and waves slowly break it into smaller and smaller pieces called microplastics. These tiny pieces are so small that fish, and even the plankton at the bottom of the ocean food chain, can accidentally swallow them.
Consider one example: a study found microplastics inside the stomachs of seabirds thousands of kilometers from the nearest city. This shows just how far ocean plastic can travel and how deeply it has entered the natural world, far beyond where people actually throw it away.
Because humans eat fish and seafood, some of this plastic may eventually end up on our own dinner tables. Scientists are still studying exactly how this affects human health, but many agree that reducing plastic waste is an urgent task.
Some countries have begun banning single-use plastic bags and straws. Individuals can also help by carrying reusable bottles and bags, and by properly recycling plastic instead of throwing it away.

(D) A type of seaweed found in polluted water

title: The Old Fisherman and the Typhoon (故事)
passage:
Old Ah-Gong had fished along the same stretch of Taiwan's coast for over forty years. He knew the sea the way other people know their own homes — its moods, its warnings, its quiet moments before danger.
One August afternoon, the sky turned a strange shade of yellow-gray. Ah-Gong noticed the birds flying inland earlier than usual and the waves rolling in with an odd, heavy rhythm. Younger fishermen laughed and said the weather report showed clear skies for two more days.
Ah-Gong did not argue. He simply pulled his boat further up the beach, tied it with extra rope, and told his grandson to help the neighbors secure their boats too. By evening, the wind had already picked up.
The typhoon arrived early the next morning, far ahead of what the official forecast had predicted. Several boats that were left in the water were damaged, but Ah-Gong's boat, along with the ones his grandson had helped secure, remained safe.
Afterward, the younger fishermen visited Ah-Gong, no longer laughing. They asked him to teach them how to read the sky and the sea the way he did, so they could learn to trust more than just a forecast.

title: The Science of Laughter (說明文)
passage:
Laughter seems like such a simple, everyday thing that we rarely stop to ask why humans do it at all. Yet scientists who study laughter have discovered that it is far more complex than most people assume, and it may have played a surprising role in human survival.
Unlike we might expect, most laughter has little to do with jokes. Researchers who recorded thousands of hours of everyday conversations found that people usually laugh at ordinary comments, not clever punchlines. A simple remark like "I'll see you later" can trigger genuine laughter if it is said between close friends in a relaxed moment. This suggests that laughter is mainly a social signal, one that says, "I feel safe and comfortable with you," rather than a direct response to something objectively funny.
Consider, for instance, how rarely people laugh when they are completely alone. Studies show that people are up to thirty times more likely to laugh in the company of others than by themselves, even when watching the exact same funny video. This striking difference reveals that laughter functions largely as a tool for building and strengthening social bonds between people, rather than a private reaction to humor alone.
Laughter can also be surprisingly contagious. Hearing someone else laugh often triggers a similar response in our own brains, even before we know what is funny. Some scientists believe this ability evolved because groups who could bond quickly through shared laughter cooperated better and survived more easily.
So the next time you laugh with friends, remember that you are doing something deeply human — building connection, one laugh at a time.

title: Learning to Surf in Yilan (故事)
passage:
Growing up far from the coast, Ting-Wei had never even touched the ocean until his family moved to Yilan the summer before ninth grade. His new classmates spent their weekends surfing, and although the idea both excited and terrified him, he finally agreed to try it after weeks of gentle encouragement.
His first attempt was, in his own words, "a complete disaster." He swallowed what felt like half the ocean, lost his balance instantly, and watched his board float away twice within the first ten minutes. Some of the more experienced kids laughed, though not unkindly, and one of them, a girl named Amy, paddled over to help him climb back on.
"Everyone falls at first," Amy told him, steadying his board. "The trick is not to fight the wave — you're supposed to move with it, not against it." Ting-Wei tried to remember her words as wave after wave knocked him down that afternoon.
By his fifth weekend of practice, something finally clicked. As a modest wave rose behind him, he pushed himself up, wobbled for a heart-stopping second, and then, impossibly, found his balance. He rode the wave all the way to the shallow water, grinning so widely that his cheeks hurt.
That night, writing in his journal, Ting-Wei realized surfing had taught him something beyond balance. Whenever life knocked him down afterward, he found himself thinking of Amy's advice: stop struggling against the wave, and instead, learn to move with it.

title: A Letter to My Future Self (故事)
passage:
Our English teacher gave us an unusual assignment: write a letter to yourself, to be opened five years later. Most of my classmates groaned, complaining that they had no idea what to say to a version of themselves that didn't exist yet. I felt the same way, staring at a blank page for nearly twenty minutes before writing a single word.
Eventually, I decided to write about my fears rather than my hopes. I admitted, in ink, that I was terrified of failing my high school entrance exam, that I doubted whether I was smart enough for the school I wanted, and that I often compared myself unfavorably to my older brother, who had always been the confident, capable one in our family.
I sealed the letter and mostly forgot about it, buried under years of homework, friendships, and the ordinary chaos of growing up.
Five years later, our teacher, now retired, mailed the letters back to us exactly as promised. Reading my own handwriting again felt strange, almost like eavesdropping on a stranger. The exam I had feared so deeply had happened, and passed, years ago, its outcome barely memorable now. My old worries about my brother had quietly faded as I found my own path, separate from his.
What struck me most wasn't how much had changed, but how much unnecessary worry I had carried at fourteen, over things that would eventually resolve themselves. I wished I could reach back through the years and tell that anxious version of myself to relax, just a little.

title: Should School Uniforms Be Required? — Two Views (雙方觀點)
passage:
■ Article 1 — By Jason Huang, Grade 9 Student

I believe school uniforms should not be required. When students can choose their own clothes, they can express their personalities and feel more like individuals rather than identical copies of each other. Getting dressed for school can even be a small daily joy, a chance to feel confident before facing a busy day. Some people argue that uniforms prevent unfair comparisons between rich and poor students, but I think this problem exists with or without uniforms — students still notice differences in shoes, bags, or phones. Removing uniforms wouldn't remove those comparisons; it would simply remove one more form of self-expression that students actually enjoy.

■ Article 2 — By Ms. Wendy Chen, School Counselor

As a school counselor, I have seen how uniforms can actually reduce daily stress for many students. Without uniforms, some students spend considerable time and energy each morning worrying about what to wear, and worse, comparing their clothing brands to their classmates'. This can create real pressure, especially for students from families with limited money. Uniforms allow students to focus their morning energy on schoolwork instead of appearance. They also create a sense of belonging to the same community. While I understand students want to express individuality, I believe there are many other ways to do so — through hobbies, artwork, or friendships — that do not require expensive clothing.

title: How Coral Reefs Protect Coastlines (說明文)
passage:
Coral reefs are often celebrated for their dazzling colors and the astonishing variety of marine life they support, but many people overlook one of their most practical functions: protecting coastal communities from the raw power of the ocean.
When large waves generated by storms or typhoons approach a shoreline, a healthy reef acts much like a natural breakwater, absorbing and dispersing much of the wave's energy before it ever reaches the beach. Research conducted across several tropical regions has estimated that reefs can reduce wave energy by as much as ninety percent, a figure that becomes especially significant when a powerful storm bears down on a low-lying coastal village with no other form of natural defense.
Consider the contrast between two neighboring coastlines: one still protected by a largely intact reef, and another where the reef has been damaged by pollution, overfishing, or careless boat anchors. During the same storm, the community behind the healthy reef often experiences comparatively minor flooding, whereas the community behind the damaged reef may suffer severe erosion and structural damage to homes built close to the shore. This side-by-side comparison illustrates, more persuasively than statistics alone, exactly how much protection a healthy reef quietly provides.
Unfortunately, reefs worldwide are under serious threat from rising ocean temperatures, which can cause a damaging process known as coral bleaching, as well as from pollution and destructive fishing practices. When a reef dies, coastal communities do not just lose a beautiful underwater landscape; they lose a natural barrier that has protected them, often for generations, without anyone fully realizing it.
Protecting coral reefs, therefore, is not merely an environmental concern reserved for scientists and conservationists. It is also a matter of practical safety for millions of people who happen to live near the coast.

title: The Night the Power Went Out (故事)
passage:
The typhoon warning had been broadcast all day, but nobody in the Lin family expected the power to actually go out — not in a modern apartment building in the middle of the city. Yet at exactly 9:47 p.m., the lights flickered twice and then died completely, plunging the entire building into an unfamiliar, thick darkness.
At first, twelve-year-old Wei-Chen was thrilled. School the next day would surely be cancelled, and the sudden darkness felt like an adventure rather than an inconvenience. His mother, however, moved calmly and quickly, retrieving candles, a flashlight, and a battery-powered radio from a cabinet that Wei-Chen hadn't even known existed until that moment.
As the family gathered around a single candle in the living room, something unexpected happened: they began talking, really talking, in a way that rarely occurred when the television and their phones competed for attention. Wei-Chen's father told stories about his own childhood, describing typhoons from decades earlier when power outages were far more common and families routinely gathered exactly like this.
A knock came at the door around 10:30. It was their elderly neighbor, Mrs. Chen, who lived alone and had run out of candles. Wei-Chen's mother invited her in immediately, and soon the neighbor was sharing tea and stories with them as though she had always been part of the family.
By the time the power returned near midnight, nobody rushed to turn on the television or check their phones right away. Instead, they sat together a while longer, reluctant to let the strange, quiet magic of the evening end so quickly. Wei-Chen later admitted that the night without power had somehow felt more connected than countless ordinary evenings with electricity.

title: Is Social Media Good for Teenagers? — Two Views (雙方觀點)
passage:
■ Article 1 — By Dr. Karen Wu, Child Psychologist

Based on years of clinical research, I am increasingly convinced that unrestricted social media use poses serious risks for teenage mental health. Adolescence is already a period of intense self-comparison, and social media platforms, by design, amplify this tendency by presenting an endless stream of curated, idealized images of other people's lives. Numerous studies have linked heavy social media use among teenagers to increased rates of anxiety, poor sleep, and lower self-esteem. Furthermore, algorithms are specifically engineered to maximize the time users spend scrolling, not to protect their psychological wellbeing. While I do not advocate banning social media entirely, I strongly believe parents and schools must actively guide teenagers toward healthier, more limited usage patterns.

■ Article 2 — By Marcus Lin, High School Student and Blogger

As someone who grew up entirely within the social media era, I find these warnings somewhat exaggerated and disconnected from how my generation actually experiences these platforms. For many of us, social media is not primarily a source of anxiety but rather an essential tool for maintaining friendships, discovering communities of people who share our specific interests, and even learning new skills through educational content. Teenagers dealing with isolation, particularly those in rural areas or those who feel different from their immediate peers, often find genuine, supportive communities online that they simply cannot access locally. Rather than treating all social media use as inherently harmful, adults should focus on teaching digital literacy skills so that we can navigate these platforms wisely and safely on our own.

options: (A) Protect users' mental health above all else
(B) Maximize the time users spend scrolling

Panel 1: Two students, Amy and Ben, accidentally activate an old, mysterious clock in their school's storage room and suddenly find themselves standing in a bustling Taiwanese street market from decades earlier, surrounded by unfamiliar sounds and smells, uncertain of what has just happened to them.
Panel 2: An elderly street vendor notices their confused, modern clothing and, rather than being alarmed, kindly offers them a traditional snack, patiently explaining a local custom that neither Amy nor Ben has ever heard of before, despite having lived in Taiwan their whole lives.

title: Night Owls and Early Birds (說明文)
passage:
Some people jump out of bed at six in the morning full of energy. Others feel half asleep until noon but come alive late at night. Scientists call these two types "early birds" and "night owls," and the difference is not just about habit — it is partly written in our genes.
Inside our bodies, a kind of internal clock controls when we feel sleepy and when we feel awake. This clock is called the circadian rhythm. For early birds, the clock makes them feel tired earlier in the evening and ready to wake up before sunrise. For night owls, the same clock runs a few hours later, so they feel most alert in the late afternoon or evening.
This difference can cause problems for night owls, especially students. Most schools start early in the morning, which does not match a night owl's natural clock. As a result, many night owls feel tired and cannot focus well during morning classes, even if they went to bed at a normal time.
Scientists suggest that understanding your own body clock can help you plan your day better. A night owl might study best after dinner, while an early bird might do better finishing homework right after school. Instead of fighting against your natural rhythm, working with it can make learning easier.

title: Should Students Wear School Uniforms? — Two Views (雙方觀點)
passage:
■ View A — By Grace Huang, Grade 8 Student

I believe school uniforms are a good idea. When everyone wears the same clothes, no one feels bad about not having expensive brand-name clothing. Before my school started using uniforms, some students often talked about who wore the newest shoes or the most fashionable jacket. Now, we spend less time worrying about clothes and more time focusing on our studies. Uniforms also make it easy to get ready in the morning — I do not need to spend time choosing an outfit every day.

■ View B — By Mr. Chen, Parent

I do not agree with strict uniform rules. Clothing is one of the few ways young people can express who they are. When students are forced to wear exactly the same thing every day, they lose a small but important chance to show their own style and personality. I also think uniforms can be uncomfortable, especially in hot weather, and buying new uniforms every year can be expensive for some families. Schools should trust students to dress neatly without forcing everyone into the same clothes.

title: Learning to Surf (故事)
passage:
The first time I stood on a surfboard, I fell into the water within two seconds. My cousin Jay, who had been surfing for years, laughed and told me not to give up. "Everyone falls at the beginning," he said. "The ocean is a patient teacher."
For the rest of that summer morning, I tried again and again. My arms grew tired from paddling, and salt water stung my eyes. Each time a wave pushed me off the board, I climbed back on without thinking twice. Somewhere between the tenth and twentieth fall, something changed. I stopped worrying about falling and simply focused on the feeling of the water beneath the board.
Then it happened. A gentle wave lifted me up, and for a few seconds, I actually stood and rode it all the way to the shore. My legs shook, but I was smiling so wide that my face hurt. Jay cheered from the water.
That day taught me something I still remember: skills worth having rarely come easily. It is the falling, not just the standing, that makes the moment on the board feel so good.

title: The Power of a Short Nap (說明文)
passage:
In many countries, taking a nap in the middle of the day is seen as a habit only for babies or the elderly. But science tells a different story: a short nap can be one of the most useful tools for staying sharp and healthy.
Studies show that a nap of just twenty minutes can improve memory, mood, and attention. During this short rest, the brain gets a chance to organize information from the morning and prepare for the rest of the day. People who take short naps often make fewer mistakes and react more quickly than those who do not.
However, timing matters. Experts suggest napping in the early afternoon, usually between 1:00 and 3:00 p.m. Napping too late in the day can make it harder to fall asleep at night. Length also matters — a nap that lasts more than thirty minutes may leave a person feeling groggy instead of refreshed, because the body starts entering a deeper stage of sleep.
Some companies now provide nap rooms for workers, understanding that a well-rested employee is often a more productive one. Instead of seeing naps as a sign of laziness, we might start seeing them as a smart way to recharge.

(B) Memory, mood, and attention
(C) Eyesight only
(D) The ability to speak new languages

title: Why Does Bread Rise? (說明文)
passage:
Have you ever watched bread dough grow bigger and bigger before it goes into the oven? This magic is caused by tiny living things called yeast.
Yeast is a kind of fungus, too small to see with our eyes. When yeast is mixed with flour, water, and a little sugar, it starts to "eat" the sugar. As it eats, the yeast produces a gas called carbon dioxide. This gas forms small bubbles inside the dough, and the bubbles push the dough to expand, making it rise.
Warm temperatures help yeast work faster, which is why bakers often place dough in a warm spot to rise. If the dough is too cold, the yeast becomes slow and sleepy, and the bread will not rise well. If it is too hot, however, the yeast can die.
Once the dough is baked in the oven, the heat kills the yeast and the gas bubbles become fixed in place. That is why baked bread has all those small holes inside — each one is a tiny footprint left by hardworking yeast.

(B) Carbon dioxide gas
(C) Extra flour
(D) Cold water
questions.explanation: 文中說酵母吃糖後 produces a gas called carbon dioxide，故選 B。

(C) It stops the dough from getting moldy
(D) It helps the flour turn white

optionsZh: 溫暖的溫度讓酵母作用更快

title: The Silent Reading Hour (故事)
passage:
At first, Owen hated Silent Reading Hour. Every Friday afternoon, his teacher, Ms. Park, turned off the lights, put on soft music, and told the class to read anything they liked for forty-five minutes. Owen usually just stared at the ceiling and waited for time to pass.
One Friday, Owen forgot his phone at home and had nothing else to do. Bored, he picked up a comic book about a boy who could talk to animals. Within a few pages, he was completely lost in the story. He barely noticed when Ms. Park announced that time was up.
After that day, Owen started bringing a different book every Friday. He read about explorers, robots, and even a girl who solved mysteries in her neighborhood. He began looking forward to Fridays instead of dreading them.
By the end of the school year, Owen had read more books than anyone else in his class. When his mother asked what changed his mind about reading, he laughed and said, "I guess I just needed to find the right story."

title: Why Do Onions Make You Cry? (說明文)
passage:
Cutting an onion is a common kitchen task, but it often comes with an unwanted side effect: tears. Why does something we plan to eat make us cry before it even reaches the table?
The answer lies inside the onion's cells. When you cut an onion, you break open tiny cells that release a special chemical. This chemical quickly turns into a gas that floats up into the air and reaches your eyes. Once the gas touches your eyes, it mixes with the water there and forms a mild acid. Your eyes sense this as something that can hurt them, so they immediately make tears to wash it away.
There are a few simple tricks that may reduce tears while cutting onions. Chilling the onion in the refrigerator before cutting can slow down the chemical reaction. Cutting the onion near running water or a fan can also help carry the gas away before it reaches your eyes. Some people even wear goggles, similar to swimming goggles, to protect their eyes completely.
So the next time an onion makes you cry, remember: it is not really sad at all. It is simply chemistry doing its job.

optionsZh: 釋放出的化學物質變成氣體

title: The New Neighbor (故事)
passage:
When the moving truck parked next door, Emma peeked through the curtains, curious about who would live there. An old man got out slowly, carrying a small box of plants. He looked tired but smiled at the empty street.
For the first week, Emma only waved from a distance. But one afternoon, she noticed the old man struggling to carry a heavy bag of soil into his yard. Without thinking, she ran over and offered to help. "Thank you, young lady," he said, wiping his forehead. "I'm Mr. Alvarez. I used to be a farmer, and I still can't live without a garden."
Over the next few weeks, Emma often stopped by after school to help water the plants. Mr. Alvarez taught her the names of vegetables she had never heard of and told stories about his old farm far away. In return, Emma brought him cookies her mother baked.
By summer, Mr. Alvarez's yard was full of colorful vegetables, and Emma had learned more about gardening than she ever expected. What started as a simple wave from a window had grown into a real friendship.

title: The Science Behind Procrastination (說明文)
passage:
Almost everyone has put off an important task until the last minute, even when they knew it was a bad idea. This habit is called procrastination, and scientists say it is not really about being lazy.
Research shows that procrastination is closely connected to how our brains handle emotions. When we think about a difficult or boring task, our brain often feels a small amount of stress or discomfort. To avoid this uncomfortable feeling, the brain looks for something more enjoyable to do instead, like checking a phone or watching videos. In this way, procrastination is less about time management and more about managing emotions.
Interestingly, waiting until the last minute can sometimes work — the pressure of a close deadline pushes some people to focus quickly. However, this approach often causes more stress and lower quality work over time.
Experts suggest breaking a big task into smaller steps to make it feel less overwhelming. Starting with just five minutes of work, rather than trying to finish everything at once, can trick the brain into feeling less uncomfortable and help a person get started.

■ View A — By Ryan Tsai, Grade 9 Student

Our school starts at 7:30 a.m., and I think that is far too early. Studies show that teenagers naturally fall asleep later than adults and need more sleep at night. When school starts so early, most students do not get enough rest, which makes it hard to focus during morning classes. I often feel half asleep until third period. If school started even one hour later, students would arrive more awake and ready to learn.

■ View B — By Ms. Torres, School Principal

While I understand students' concerns about sleep, starting school later creates other problems. Many parents need to drop their children off before going to work, and a later start time would make this difficult. Bus schedules, after-school activities, and sports practices are also carefully planned around the current schedule. Changing the start time would affect many families and require reorganizing the entire school day. Instead, I encourage students to manage their bedtime better and avoid using phones late at night.

title: The Invention of the Zipper (說明文)
passage:
Today, zippers are found on almost everything — jackets, bags, boots, and pencil cases. But before zippers existed, people had to rely on buttons, hooks, and laces to close their clothing, which took much more time.
The zipper was not invented all at once. In 1893, an American engineer created an early version, but it did not work very well and often came apart. It took another inventor, Gideon Sundback, to improve the design in 1913. His version had small teeth that locked together smoothly, much like the zippers we use today.
At first, people were not sure about this strange new invention. Zippers became popular only after companies started using them on boots and tobacco pouches, showing customers how quick and convenient they were. By the 1930s, clothing designers began adding zippers to jackets and pants, and the invention quickly spread around the world.
What seems like a simple, ordinary object today actually took decades of testing and improvement. The next time you zip up a jacket in seconds, remember that this convenience was once considered a bold new idea.

title: Grandpa's Balcony Garden (故事)
passage:
My grandfather lived in a small apartment with only a narrow balcony, but somehow he grew tomatoes, peppers, and herbs in pots lined up along the railing. Every summer when I visited, he would hand me a small watering can and say, "The plants missed you."
At first, I thought it was strange that a grown man cared so much about a few pots of vegetables. But Grandpa always seemed happiest when he was out there, gently touching the leaves and checking for new buds. He told me that gardening reminded him of his childhood on a real farm, before his family moved to the city.
One summer, a terrible storm knocked over most of his pots. I expected him to be upset, but he just smiled and said, "We'll plant again. That's what gardens teach you — things grow back if you keep trying."
I am older now, living far from that small balcony, but whenever something in my life goes wrong, I remember his words. Somewhere in a tiny apartment, an old man once taught me that patience and hope can grow in even the smallest space.

title: Museum Scavenger Hunt (活動說明)

NATIONAL SCIENCE MUSEUM — Family Scavenger Hunt!
Every weekend, join our free scavenger hunt and explore the museum in a fun way!

2. Find 8 hidden clues around the museum before 4:30 p.m.

title: Why Are Some People Left-Handed? (說明文)
passage:
Most people use their right hand for writing, throwing, and eating, but about one out of every ten people naturally prefers their left hand. For a long time, scientists were not sure why this difference exists.
One clue comes from the brain. The human brain has two sides, and each side controls different tasks. For most people, the left side of the brain, which controls the right hand, is stronger at controlling detailed movements. For left-handed people, this pattern is often reversed or mixed, though scientists still do not fully understand why.
In the past, being left-handed was sometimes seen as unusual or even wrong. In some schools long ago, left-handed children were forced to write with their right hand instead, even though it felt uncomfortable and unnatural to them. Thankfully, most schools today allow students to use whichever hand feels natural.
Interestingly, research has found that left-handed people may have some advantages in certain sports, such as fencing or baseball, because their opponents are less used to facing a left-handed player. Being left-handed is simply a different way the brain and body work together — not something better or worse than being right-handed.

title: The Broken Umbrella (故事)
passage:
It was pouring rain when Wendy realized her umbrella had a broken rib, flopping uselessly to one side. She stood under the bus stop shelter, watching water drip through the gap, and wondered how she would make it to school dry.
A boy from her class, Peter, stood nearby holding a large blue umbrella. He noticed her staring at the sky and said, "You can share mine if you're heading toward the school." Wendy hesitated. She barely knew Peter, but the rain was not stopping anytime soon.
They walked together, careful to keep both of them under the umbrella. Peter talked about a video game he was designing for a school project, and Wendy found herself laughing at his jokes about his poorly drawn characters. By the time they reached the school gate, her shoes were wet, but she felt strangely cheerful.
"Thanks for sharing," Wendy said. "I owe you one."
"No problem," Peter replied. "Maybe you can help me draw better characters sometime."
Wendy smiled, realizing that a rainy morning had turned into the start of an unexpected friendship.

title: The Science of Blushing (說明文)
passage:
When someone feels embarrassed, their cheeks often turn pink or red. This reaction, known as blushing, happens so quickly that most people cannot control it, even when they want to.
Blushing is caused by the body's stress response. When we feel embarrassed, our brain sends a signal that makes blood vessels near the surface of the skin widen. More blood flows close to the skin, especially in the face, which causes the reddish color. Interestingly, the face has more blood vessels close to the surface than most other parts of the body, which is why blushing usually shows on the cheeks rather than the arms or legs.
Scientists have long wondered why humans evolved this strange, uncontrollable reaction. One theory suggests that blushing may actually be helpful. When someone blushes after making a mistake, it can silently signal to others, "I know I did something wrong, and I feel bad about it." This may help repair social relationships, since people often forgive someone who appears to feel true embarrassment.
So the next time your face turns red in front of others, remember that this reaction, though uncomfortable, might actually be helping you connect with the people around you.

title: A Trip to the Night Zoo (故事)
passage:
Most people visit the zoo during the day, but on my tenth birthday, my parents surprised me with tickets to the Night Zoo — a special event held only twice a year. I had never imagined the zoo could look and feel so different after dark.
As soon as we walked in, the paths were lit only by small lanterns, and the air felt cooler and quieter than usual. Animals that hid during the day were suddenly wide awake. We watched owls silently spread their wings and heard the low calls of animals I had never even heard about before.
My favorite part was the flashlight tour through the nocturnal animal house, where a guide showed us bats hanging upside down and a slow-moving creature called a slow loris with enormous round eyes. The guide explained that many animals are actually more active at night to avoid the heat and hide from predators.
By the time we left, my legs were tired, but my mind was full of new questions about animals I had never noticed before. I realized the zoo I thought I already knew still had entire hidden worlds waiting to be discovered.

title: Should Homework Be Banned? — Two Views (雙方觀點)
passage:
■ View A — By Sophia Lin, Grade 8 Student

I think schools should get rid of homework, or at least give much less of it. After a full day of classes, my brain feels tired, and homework only adds more stress to my evenings. I also believe homework does not always help students learn — sometimes I just copy answers to finish quickly instead of really understanding the material. If schools gave less homework, students would have more time to rest, play sports, and spend time with family, which are all important parts of growing up.

■ View B — By Mr. Patel, Math Teacher

Homework plays an important role in learning, especially for subjects like math and language. Class time is often too short to fully practice a new skill, and homework gives students a chance to review what they learned and find out what they still do not understand. Without practice at home, many students would forget new material quickly. Of course, homework should be reasonable in amount, but removing it completely would likely hurt students' long-term learning.

title: The Search for Biscuit (故事)
passage:
When Ella's dog, Biscuit, slipped out of the front gate one evening, panic set in immediately. Biscuit had never run away before, and Ella had no idea which direction he might have gone.
Her family split up to search different streets, calling Biscuit's name into the dark. Ella printed pictures of Biscuit on her phone and showed them to anyone she passed, asking if they had seen a small brown dog with a red collar. Most people shook their heads, but one elderly woman said she had seen a dog matching that description near the park about an hour earlier.
Ella ran toward the park, her heart pounding with hope. Under a bench near the pond, she spotted a familiar shape shivering in the cold. "Biscuit!" she shouted, and the dog's ears perked up immediately before he ran straight into her arms.
Walking home with Biscuit safely on his leash, Ella promised herself she would always double-check the gate from now on. Some lessons, she realized, were worth learning the hard way.

title: The Art of Origami (說明文)
passage:
With nothing but a single square piece of paper, some people can create a graceful crane, a roaring lion, or even a tiny working box. This traditional art of paper folding, called origami, began in Japan hundreds of years ago and has since spread across the world.
What makes origami special is that it uses no scissors and no glue — only careful folds transform a flat sheet into a three-dimensional shape. Each fold must be precise, because even a small mistake early on can change the entire final shape. This is why origami is often used to teach patience and attention to detail, especially to children.
Origami is not just for fun. Engineers and scientists have started studying paper-folding techniques to solve real-world problems. For example, some space equipment is designed using origami methods so that large solar panels can be folded into a small, compact shape for launch, and then unfold once they reach space.
What began as a simple form of art has grown into a bridge between tradition and modern technology, showing that even the humblest materials can inspire powerful ideas.

title: The Day I Rode Alone (故事)
passage:
For weeks, my father held onto the back of my bicycle seat every time I practiced, running beside me down our street. I felt safe knowing he was there, but I never actually learned to balance on my own — I always relied on his hand keeping me steady.
One Saturday morning, I turned around expecting to see him jogging behind me, but the street behind me was empty. I had been riding by myself for almost a whole block without realizing it. My heart jumped, and out of surprise, I nearly fell over.
"You did it!" my father called from where he had quietly let go minutes earlier. He had been walking, not running, the entire time.
At first, I felt a little tricked, but then a wave of pride washed over me. I pedaled harder, circling the block twice just to prove to myself that it was not luck. When I finally stopped in front of our house, my legs were shaking, but I was grinning from ear to ear.
That small, wobbly ride taught me something bigger than balance: sometimes we are more capable than we realize, especially when someone believes in us quietly from a distance.

title: Why Do We Get Goosebumps? (說明文)
passage:
When you feel cold, scared, or even deeply moved by music, small bumps sometimes appear on your skin, and the tiny hairs on your arms stand up straight. This reaction is called goosebumps, and it is actually left over from our animal ancestors.
Long ago, early humans had much more body hair than we do today. When they felt cold, tiny muscles at the base of each hair would tighten, making the hair stand up. This trapped a layer of warm air close to the skin, helping to keep the body warm, similar to how a cat's fur puffs up in cold weather.
Goosebumps also appeared when early humans felt frightened. Standing hair made the body look bigger, which may have helped scare away enemies or predators. Even though modern humans have much less body hair, the same muscles under our skin still react the same way.
Interestingly, goosebumps can also happen when we listen to powerful music or watch an emotional movie scene. Scientists believe this happens because strong emotions activate the same nervous system pathway as fear or cold, even though there is no real danger or chill involved.

title: The School Newspaper Club (故事)
passage:
Nora joined the school newspaper club mostly because her best friend asked her to, not because she thought she had anything interesting to write about. During the first meeting, the club leader asked everyone to pitch a story idea, and Nora's mind went completely blank while other students listed exciting topics one after another.
That weekend, while walking home, Nora noticed an elderly man feeding stray cats behind the school every evening. Curious, she stopped to ask him about it. He explained that he had been doing this quietly for almost ten years, never telling anyone, simply because he could not stand to see hungry animals.
Nora wrote his story carefully, trying to capture how ordinary the man seemed even though his small habit meant so much to the neighborhood cats. When the article was published, several students told her it was their favorite piece in the whole newspaper.
Nora realized that she did not need a dramatic topic to write something meaningful. Sometimes the most powerful stories were hiding quietly in places people walked past every day without noticing.

title: The History of the Traffic Light (說明文)
passage:
Before cars filled the streets, cities were already struggling with traffic problems caused by horses, carriages, and pedestrians all sharing the same busy roads. The very first traffic signal, installed in London in 1868, was actually designed for this earlier kind of traffic, not cars.
This early signal used gas lamps and had to be operated by a police officer standing beside it, turning the light by hand. Unfortunately, the gas lamp exploded only a few weeks after it was installed, injuring the officer and causing the project to be abandoned for many years.
As cars became more common in the early 1900s, the need for a safer traffic system returned. In 1920, an American police officer named William Potts is often credited with creating the modern three-color traffic light — red, yellow, and green — in Detroit. His design used electricity instead of gas, making it far safer and easier to maintain.
Today, traffic lights are found in almost every city around the world, quietly managing the flow of millions of vehicles every day. What began as a dangerous experiment eventually became one of the most important inventions for keeping city streets safe.

options: (A) It worked perfectly for many years
(B) The gas lamp exploded, injuring the officer
(C) It was replaced by a modern light immediately
(D) It was moved to another country
questions.explanation: 文中說 the gas lamp exploded only a few weeks after it was installed, injuring the officer，故選 B。
optionsZh: 瓦斯燈爆炸，使警官受傷

questions.explanation: 文中說 William Potts is often credited with creating the modern three-color traffic light... in Detroit，故選 B。
optionsZh: 底特律的警官 William Potts

title: My Robot Vacuum's First Week (故事)
passage:
My family got a robot vacuum for the living room, and I was convinced it would make my life easier. Instead, its first week at home turned into a series of small disasters that had the whole family laughing.
On the very first day, the little machine got its wheels tangled in a phone charger cable and spent twenty minutes spinning in circles, beeping helplessly until my sister rescued it. The next day, it discovered my shoes by the door and pushed one all the way into the kitchen, as if it had decided the shoe did not belong there.
By the third day, it had somehow gotten stuck under the couch and refused to come out, making a sad little humming sound until my father lifted the couch to free it. My mother joked that our new robot had more personality than our old vacuum ever did.
By the end of the week, though, something changed. It began avoiding the charger cable, learned the shape of our furniture, and cleaned the floor without a single mistake. Watching it glide smoothly across the living room, I realized that even machines, like people, sometimes need time to learn.

title: The Secret of Sourdough Bread (說明文)
passage:
Sourdough bread has a unique tangy taste and a chewy texture that many people love, but what makes it different from ordinary bread is not a special ingredient — it is the absence of one.
Most bread recipes use commercial yeast, a product bought in a package, to help dough rise. Sourdough bread, however, relies on a natural mixture of wild yeast and bacteria called a "starter." This starter is made by simply mixing flour and water and leaving it out at room temperature for several days. Over time, wild yeast that naturally exists in the air and on the flour begins to grow inside the mixture.
Caring for a sourdough starter takes patience. It must be "fed" regularly with more flour and water, or the wild yeast inside will grow weak and stop working properly. Many bakers give their starters nicknames and treat them almost like pets, since a healthy starter can be kept alive and used for years, even decades.
The slow, natural fermentation process is also what gives sourdough its distinct sour flavor and helps make the bread easier to digest for some people. In a world of fast food, sourdough bread reminds us that some of the best things still take time.

title: The Kind Stranger at the Bus Stop (故事)
passage:
It was already dark when I realized I had left my wallet at home, standing alone at the bus stop with no way to pay for my ride. I checked my bag three times, hoping the wallet would magically appear, but of course it did not.
An older woman sitting on the bench noticed my worried face. "Are you all right?" she asked gently. I explained my situation, feeling embarrassed to admit such a simple mistake to a stranger. Without hesitation, she opened her purse and handed me enough coins for the bus fare.
"You can pay it forward someday," she said with a warm smile, waving away my thanks. "That's how I got through some hard days when I was young."
I rode the bus home that night thinking about her words. A few weeks later, I saw a young student at the same bus stop, frantically searching his pockets for money. Without a second thought, I offered him some coins, remembering the kindness of a stranger who had once done the same for me.
That small chain of kindness, passed from one stranger to another, taught me that even the smallest gesture can travel farther than we imagine.

title: Do Fish Really Sleep? (說明文)
passage:
It might seem like a strange question, but scientists have long studied whether fish actually sleep, since most fish do not have eyelids and cannot close their eyes like humans do.
The answer is yes — fish do sleep, but in a way that looks quite different from human sleep. Instead of lying down and closing their eyes, many fish enter a resting state where their movement slows down greatly, their breathing becomes steady, and they become less responsive to what is happening around them. Some fish rest near the bottom of the water, some float in place, and a few even find a safe spot between rocks or coral to stay still.
Interestingly, certain kinds of fish, like parrotfish, create a soft, jelly-like layer around their bodies at night, believed to help hide their scent from predators while they rest. Other fish, such as some sharks, must keep swimming even while resting because they need constant water flow over their gills to breathe.
So the next time you look at a fish tank and see the fish floating quietly, it might not be simply relaxing — it could actually be fast asleep.
