title: How Fireworks Get Their Colors (說明文)
passage:
Every festival night, the sky lights up with bursts of red, green, blue, and gold, but few people stop to wonder how fireworks create such vivid colors. The secret lies in chemistry.
Inside every firework are tiny packets of chemical compounds called metal salts. When a firework explodes, the intense heat causes these metal salts to burn and release energy in the form of light. Different metals produce different colors when burned. For example, strontium salts create bright red light, copper compounds produce blue, and sodium creates a strong yellow-orange glow, similar to the color of some streetlights.
Creating a firework with the perfect color and timing takes great skill. Firework makers must carefully calculate the exact amount of each chemical, as well as how the explosion is shaped, to control both the color and the pattern of light in the sky. Blue fireworks, interestingly, are considered the hardest to create because the chemical reaction for producing blue light is more delicate and easily disturbed by other elements in the mixture.
So the next time you watch fireworks light up the night, remember that each burst of color is the result of careful chemistry, not just a beautiful accident.

title: A Night Without Electricity (故事)
passage:
The lights flickered twice before the whole house went completely dark. Outside, thunder rolled across the sky, and my phone showed almost no battery left. I groaned, imagining a boring night with nothing to do.
My little sister, however, seemed delighted. She dragged out an old box of candles and, with our mother's help, lit them one by one until the living room glowed with a soft, warm light. "It's like camping inside the house," she said, grinning in the candlelight.
With no television or phones to distract us, we ended up playing an old card game that had been sitting forgotten in a drawer for years. My father told stories about power outages when he was a child, back when storms like this happened more often. We laughed harder than we had in weeks, our voices echoing strangely in the quiet, dark house.
When the lights finally came back around midnight, no one rushed to turn on the television. We simply sat there for another moment, strangely reluctant to end the evening. I realized that sometimes losing something we depend on can accidentally give us something we did not know we were missing.

title: The Great Alarm Clock Battle (故事)
passage:
Every school morning felt like a small war between me and my alarm clock. I would set three separate alarms, five minutes apart, convinced this clever plan would finally get me out of bed on time. Instead, I simply learned to silence all three without ever fully waking up.
One morning, my older brother, tired of my constant lateness, secretly placed my phone across the room instead of on my nightstand. When the alarm rang, I had no choice but to physically get up and walk across the cold floor to turn it off. To my surprise, once I was standing, I felt too awake to crawl back into bed.
At first, I was annoyed at his little trick, but I noticed something interesting — I actually made it to breakfast on time for the first time in weeks. The next night, I placed my phone across the room myself, half expecting the plan to fail.
It did not fail. Slowly, waking up on time stopped feeling like an impossible battle. My brother just laughed and said, "Sometimes you don't need more alarms — you just need one good reason to stand up."

title: The Rise of Online Learning (說明文)
passage:
In the past, learning a new subject usually meant sitting in a classroom with a teacher and a group of classmates. Today, more and more students are choosing to learn online, using videos, apps, and websites instead of, or in addition to, traditional classrooms.
One major advantage of online learning is flexibility. Students can often study at their own pace, pausing a lesson to review a difficult part or speeding through material they already understand. This is especially helpful for students who learn faster or slower than the average pace of a classroom. Online platforms also allow students in remote areas, without access to certain schools or teachers, to learn subjects that might otherwise be unavailable to them.
However, online learning also comes with challenges. Without a teacher physically present, some students find it harder to stay focused or ask questions right away. Learning online also requires strong self-discipline, since there is no classmate or teacher directly checking whether a student is paying attention.
Many experts now believe the best approach may be a mix of both methods — combining the structure and social connection of classroom learning with the flexibility and convenience that online tools provide.

optionsZh: 可依自己的步調彈性學習

(D) It requires students to travel far away

沒有老師實際在場較難保持專注

(D) Avoiding both methods completely

optionsZh: 結合課堂學習與網路工具

title: The Mystery of Déjà Vu (說明文)
passage:
Have you ever entered a place for the first time but suddenly felt certain you had been there before? This strange, brief feeling is called déjà vu, a French phrase meaning "already seen." Studies suggest that most people experience déjà vu at least once in their lives, often during their teenage or young adult years.
Scientists do not fully agree on why déjà vu happens, but they have several interesting theories. One idea suggests that déjà vu occurs when the brain mistakenly processes a new experience using the same pathway used for memory, causing the brain to falsely label a new moment as familiar. Another theory suggests it may happen when the brain briefly experiences a tiny delay in processing information from the two eyes, creating a strange sense of repetition.
Interestingly, déjà vu tends to happen less often as people get older. Some researchers believe this may be because the brain becomes better at telling the difference between real memories and unfamiliar experiences as it matures.
While scientists continue to study this mysterious feeling, one thing is certain: déjà vu remains one of the most puzzling and fascinating tricks our brain can play on us.

questions.question: What does the phrase "déjà vu" mean?
options: (A) "Already seen"
(B) "Never before"
(C) "完全忘記"

options: (A) The brain processes a new experience using the same pathway used for memory

questions.explanation: 文中提到一種理論認為大腦用記憶的路徑處理新經驗，因而誤把新時刻標記為熟悉，故選 A。
optionsZh: 大腦用處理記憶的相同路徑來處理新經驗

(B) It happens less often
(C) It becomes more painful
(D) It disappears completely at age eighteen
questions.explanation: 文中說 déjà vu tends to happen less often as people get older，故選 B。

title: Piano Competition Nerves (故事)
passage:
Backstage at the piano competition, my hands felt cold and my stomach twisted into knots. I had practiced the same piece almost every day for three months, yet standing so close to the stage made me forget how the first note even began.
A girl waiting beside me, clearly noticing my shaking hands, offered a small piece of advice. "I used to get so nervous I'd forget entire pieces," she said calmly. "Now I just imagine I'm playing for myself, alone in my room. The audience disappears that way."
When my name was finally called, I walked onto the stage on shaky legs, sat down, and took a slow breath, trying her trick. I imagined my quiet bedroom instead of the crowded auditorium. Slowly, my fingers found the first note, then the second, and the melody began flowing almost on its own.
I made a small mistake near the end, but I kept playing, refusing to let it stop the music. When I finished, the applause felt surprisingly warm. I did not win first place that day, but I walked off stage prouder than I had ever felt — not because I played perfectly, but because I did not let fear silence the music.

(C) Skip the difficult parts of the piece
(D) Play the piece faster than usual

title: The Science Behind Fall Leaves (說明文)
passage:
Every autumn, forests transform from a sea of green into brilliant shades of red, orange, and yellow. This colorful change is not magic — it is the result of a natural process happening inside each leaf.
During spring and summer, leaves appear green because of a chemical called chlorophyll, which helps plants absorb sunlight and turn it into energy. As days grow shorter and temperatures drop in autumn, trees prepare for winter by slowing down and eventually stopping the production of chlorophyll. As the green color fades, other colors that were hiding inside the leaf the whole time finally become visible.
Yellow and orange colors come from chemicals called carotenoids, which were always present in the leaf but hidden behind the stronger green color. Red colors, however, are often created fresh in autumn, as sugars trapped inside the leaf react with sunlight and cool temperatures.
Eventually, the tree forms a special layer at the base of each leaf stem, cutting off water and nutrients completely. This causes the leaf to dry out and fall to the ground, making space for fresh new leaves to grow again in spring.

title: The Quiet Kid Who Loved to Paint (故事)
passage:
Everyone in class knew Daniel as the quiet kid who rarely spoke during group discussions and usually ate lunch alone. Few people knew that after school, he spent hours in his room, filling notebooks with detailed paintings of imaginary cities and creatures.
When our art teacher announced a school-wide art contest, most students assumed the winner would be one of the outgoing, popular students who always seemed confident. Daniel, however, quietly submitted a painting of a floating city surrounded by glowing clouds, without telling anyone in advance.
When the results were announced, the whole school gathered around the winning painting, stunned to learn it had been created by Daniel. Suddenly, classmates who had barely spoken to him before were asking questions about his painting technique and how long it had taken.
Daniel seemed surprised but pleased by the attention, though he still spoke quietly, almost shyly, about his work. Watching him explain his painting with quiet confidence, I realized that people are often far more interesting than their quiet exterior suggests — we simply have to give them the chance to show it.

title: Should Zoos Exist? — Two Views (雙方觀點)
passage:
■ View A — By Dr. Amara Osei, Wildlife Researcher

Modern zoos play an important role in protecting endangered animals. Many species facing extinction in the wild are being carefully bred in zoos, keeping their populations alive until it may be safe to return them to nature. Zoos also allow millions of children and adults to see animals up close, which often creates a lasting interest in protecting wildlife and the environment. Without this personal connection, many people would never feel motivated to support conservation efforts happening far away from their own cities.

■ View B — By Tomas Reyes, Animal Rights Activist

While the goals of zoos may sound good, keeping wild animals in enclosures, no matter how large, still limits their natural behavior and freedom. Many animals in zoos show signs of stress and boredom, repeating the same movements again and again inside small spaces. Instead of keeping animals in cities far from their natural homes, we should invest more in protecting their original habitats and supporting research in the wild. True conservation should focus on saving nature itself, not moving animals away from it.
